title: The Baker's Dozen
author: Fiona Hayes
pages:
Chapter 1: A Sweet Rivalry

Prachi breathed in the scent of fresh cinnamon and blooming freesia. She stood proudly outside her newly opened flower shop, 'Petal Pushers,' right on the quaint, cobblestone main street of Willow Creek. It was her dream realized. The shop was flooded with sunlight, bursting with vibrant arrangements and trailing ivy.

However, her neighbor was less enthusiastic. Right next door was 'Oven & Ovens,' the town's most popular, established bakery. The owner, a grumpy, broad-shouldered man named Ashish, stood glaring at her from behind a stack of sourdough loaves through his flour-dusted window.

'Morning, neighbor!' Prachi called out, waving cheerfully at him from the sidewalk. Her bright yellow sundress felt entirely out of place next to his scowl and faded gray apron.

Ashish merely grunted, turning away to violently knead a massive mound of dough on his counter. He had a reputation in Willow Creek. His pastries were legendary, his croissants flaky perfection, but his personality was as warm as a day-old baguette. He hated change. And Prachi's bright, disruptive floral business was a very big change.

Prachi sighed, tying her hair back. She marched into his bakery the next morning, determined to break the ice. The bell chimed over the door, smelling a heavenly mix of vanilla and baking bread.

'I need a bear claw,' Prachi asked brightly, leaning on the glass counter. 'And a smile, if you have one in stock.'

Ashish looked up, flour decorating his dark hair and strong jawline. His eyes were a startling, intense blue under furrowed brows. 'I sell pastries, Ms. Day. Not pleasantries. That will be three dollars.'

Chapter 2: Flour and Flowers

Their dynamic was quickly established as the town's primary source of entertainment. Prachi would leave bright, obnoxious bouquets of sunflowers on Ashish's doorstep to assert dominance. Ashish would leave slightly over-baked (but still delicious) muffins on her back step as a terrible peace offering.

The real trouble started during the annual Willow Creek Spring Festival. The town council announced a joint business initiative—stores on Main Street had to partner up for a unified display to win a massive grant.

'We are partnered, Ashish. It's the rules,' Prachi insisted, standing in his kitchen while he furiously frosted a dozen cupcakes. 'Flowers and pastries. It's a classic combination.'

Ashish stopped, dropping the piping bag. He leaned over the stainless steel table, looming over her. The kitchen was sweltering, and a bead of sweat rolled down his neck. Prachi unexpectedly found him entirely, distractingly handsome when he wasn't glaring.

'Flower petals on my tarts are a health code violation, Prachi. Your pollen is going to ruin my meringue,' he grumbled, wiping his hands on a towel. But she noticed he didn't actually ask her to leave.

'I use edible flowers,' she countered, stepping closer, holding up a small, vibrant purple pansy. She surprisingly reached up and tucked it into the pocket of his apron. 'A pop of color won't kill you, Grumpy.'

Ashish froze, looking down at the delicate flower against his rough canvas apron. For a split second, the scowl broke, and Prachi swore she saw the corner of his mouth twitch upward.

Chapter 3: The Secret Ingredient

They reluctantly began planning the display. Prachi spent her evenings in Ashish's hot, flour-dusted kitchen, helping him brainstorm flavors that matched her floral arrangements. Lavender and lemon verbena scones. Rosewater macarons. The hostility slowly melted into a comfortable, teasing banter.

Prachi learned that Ashish wasn't actually grumpy; he was just fiercely protective of his late grandmother's recipes and terrified of failing the legacy she left him. Ashish learned that Prachi's relentless sunshine was a hard-fought battle against past disappointments. They balanced each other out perfectly.

One Friday night, they were covered in powdered sugar, trying to perfect a notoriously difficult batch of elderflower croissants. Prachi was laughing uncontrollably as Ashish tried, and failed, to pipe a perfect rosette.

'Let me,' Prachi giggled, reaching over to guide his large, flour-covered hand with her own. Ashish's hand felt incredibly warm under hers. The laughter abruptly died in her throat as she looked up. He was already looking at her.

The air in the kitchen grew incredibly thick, smelling of baking butter and sugar. Ashish slowly lowered the piping bag, not breaking eye contact. He reached up, his thumb gently wiping a smudge of powdered sugar from her cheekbone.

'You have a terrible habit of invading my space, Prachi Day,' he murmured, his voice incredibly deep and entirely stripped of its usual gruffness.

'And you have a terrible habit of letting me in, Ashish,' she whispered back, leaning into his touch.

Chapter 4: A Taste of Sugar

Ashish kissed her right there in the flour-dusted kitchen. It was tentative at first, then deep and impossibly sweet, tasting like vanilla and cinnamon. The grumpy baker pulled her flush against him, wrapping his strong arms around her waist, completely destroying any pretense of rivalry.

The Spring Festival display was a triumph. The 'Oven & Ovens' and 'Petal Pushers' collaborative booth was a stunning archway of blooming vines interlaced with shelves of perfectly matched floral-infused pastries. They won the grant easily.

As the sun set over Willow Creek and the festival crowds began to thin, Ashish found Prachi sitting on a bench near her shop, exhausted but glowing with happiness. He walked over, handing her a small, perfectly crafted lavender macaron.

'We make a good team,' Ashish finally admitted, taking a bite of his own pastry and looking at her sideways.

Prachi smiled, intertwining her fingers with his. 'The best team. Even if you are still impossibly grumpy before your morning coffee.'

'I suppose I can tolerate a daily dose of sunshine,' Ashish conceded, pulling her in for another lingering kiss. The rivalry was dead. Love, much like his sourdough starter, had just needed a little time and warmth to rise.
